subject: Physics
q:
Match List - I with List - II.

List - I (Number) List - II (Signficant figure)
(A) 1001 (I) 3
(B) 010.1 (II) 4
(C) 100.100 (III) 5
(D) 0.0010010 (IV) 6

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List - I with List - II.

List - I (Number) List - II (Signficant figure)
(A) 1001 (I) 3
(B) 010.1 (II) 4
(C) 100.100 (III) 5
(D) 0.0010010 (IV) 6

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Rules for significant figures:

1. All non-zero numbers are significant

2. Zeros between two non-zero digits are significant

3. Leading zeros are not significant

4. Trailing zeros to the right of the decimal are significant

5. Trailing zeros in a whole number with the decimal shown are significant

6. Trailing zeros in a whole number with no decimal shown are not significant

From above rule, we get

(A)-(II), (B)-(I), (C)-(IV), (D)-(III)

subject: Physics
q: Resistance of a given wire is obtained by measuring the current flowing in it and the voltage difference applied across it. If the percentage errors in the measurement of the current and the voltage difference are $3 \%$ each, then error in the value of resistance of the wire is
question: Resistance of a given wire is obtained by measuring the current flowing in it and the voltage difference applied across it. If the percentage errors in the measurement of the current and the voltage difference are $3 \%$ each, then error in the value of resistance of the wire is
solution:
$\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{i}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left|\frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}}\right|=\left|\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}\right|+\left|\frac{\Delta \mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{i}}\right|$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}} \times 100=3$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}=0.03$
Similarly, $\frac{\Delta i}{i}=0.03$
Hence $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}}=0.06$
So percentage error is $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}} \times 100=6 \%$

subject: Physics
q: The relative error in the determination of the surface area of a sphere is $\alpha$. Then the relative error in the determination of its volume is
question: The relative error in the determination of the surface area of a sphere is $\alpha$. Then the relative error in the determination of its volume is
solution:
Relative error in Surface area, $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{s}}{\mathrm{s}}=2 \times \frac{\Delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}=\alpha$ and relative error in volume, $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}}=3 \times \frac{\Delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}$
$\therefore$ Relative error in volume w.r.t. relative error in area,
$$
\frac{\Delta \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{3}{2} \alpha
$$

What will be the value of its volume in appropriate significant figures?
question: The diameter and height of a cylinder are measured by a meter scale to be <math><mn>12.6</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0.1</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> and <math><mn>34.2</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0.1</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> , respectively. What will be the value of its volume in appropriate significant figures?
solution:
V=πd2h4=π412.6234.2 cm3

dVV=2ddd+dhh

Converting to errors

∆VV=2∆dd+∆hh

∆V4260=20.112.6+0.134.2

⇒∆V=80 cm3

V=4260±80 cm3

q: The period of oscillation of a simple pendulum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><msqrt><mfrac><mi>L</mi><mi>g</mi></mfrac></msqrt><mo>.</mo></math> Measured value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from meter scale having a minimum division of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and time of one complete oscillation is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>95</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> measured from stopwatch of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> resolution. The percentage error in the determination of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi>g</mi><mo>'</mo></math> will be:
question: The period of oscillation of a simple pendulum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><msqrt><mfrac><mi>L</mi><mi>g</mi></mfrac></msqrt><mo>.</mo></math> Measured value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from meter scale having a minimum division of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and time of one complete oscillation is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>95</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> measured from stopwatch of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> resolution. The percentage error in the determination of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi>g</mi><mo>'</mo></math> will be:
solution:
T=2πℓg

g=4π2ℓT2

Δgg=Δℓℓ+2ΔTT

Δgg=1×10-31+2×0.011.95

Δgg=0.0113 or 1.13%

q: A silver wire has a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>006</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>, radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>005</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>mm</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math>. The maximum percentage error in the measurement of its density will be
question: A silver wire has a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>006</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>, radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>005</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>mm</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math>. The maximum percentage error in the measurement of its density will be
solution:
Volume of the wire is V=πr2l.

Density of the wire is ρ=mπr2l

Relative error in measurement of density

⇒Δρρ=Δmm+2×Δrr+Δll

=0.0060.6+2×0.0050.5+0.044

=0.01+0.02+0.01=0.04

∴ Percentage error in ρ=0.04×100=4%

q: If the length of the pendulum in pendulum clock increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo></math>, then the error in time per day is:
question: If the length of the pendulum in pendulum clock increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo></math>, then the error in time per day is:
solution:
T=2πlg

T∝l12

ΔTT=12Δll

ΔT24×3600=12×0.1100

ΔT=12×0.1100×24×3600

ΔT=43.2 s

subject: Physics
q: The resultant of two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{A}$ and its magnitude is half that of $\vec{B}$. The angle between vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ is ______ $\circ$.
question: The resultant of two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{A}$ and its magnitude is half that of $\vec{B}$. The angle between vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ is ______ $\circ$.
solution: $\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{B} \cos \theta=\frac{\mathrm{B}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=60^{\circ}\end{aligned}$ So, angle between $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}} \& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$ is $90^{\circ}+60^{\circ}=150^{\circ}$

 Observed Value

Thickness of bar  (d) 0.01 mm 0.4 cm

Then the fractional error in the measurement of Y is :
question:
A student determined Young's Modulus of elasticity using the formula Y=MgL34bd3δ. The value of g is taken to be 9.8 m s-2 without any significant error, his observations are as following.

Physical Quantity

Least count of the Equipment used for measurement

 Observed Value
Mass (M) 1 g 2 kg
Length of bar (L) 1 mm 1 m
Breadth of bar (b) 0.1 mm 4 cm
Thickness of bar  (d) 0.01 mm 0.4 cm
Depression (δ) 0.01 mm 5 mm

Then the fractional error in the measurement of Y is :
solution:
Y=MgL34bd3δ

∆YY=∆MM+3∆l1+∆bb+3∆dd+∆δδ⇒∆YY=0.0005+3×0.001+0.0025+3×0.0025+0.002=0.0155

question: The magnitude of vectors OA→, OB→ and OC→ in the given figure are equal. The direction of OA→+OB→-OC→ with x-axis will be:
solution:
From the above diagram, the equations are, 
OA→=Rcos30∘i^+sin30∘j^

OB→=Rcos60∘i^-sin60°j^

OC→=R-cos45°i^+sin45°j^

⇒OA→+OB→−OC→=R12+32+12i^+R12−32−12j^, angle with the x-axis.

⇒tanα=12−32−1212+32+12=1−3−21+3+2

⇒α=tan−11−3−21+3+2.

subject: Physics
q: A vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is rotated by a small angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>θ</mi></math> radians <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>∆</mo><mi>θ</mi><mo>≪</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to get a new vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> . In that case <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> is :
question: A vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is rotated by a small angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>θ</mi></math> radians <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>∆</mo><mi>θ</mi><mo>≪</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to get a new vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> . In that case <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> is :
solution:
B→=A→

B→-A→=A→2+A→2+2A→A→cos180°-∆θ

=A→2+A→2-2A→2cos∆θ

=2A→2-2A→2cos∆θ

=2A→21-cos∆θ

∵1-cos∆θ=2sin2∆θ2

=2A→2×2sin2∆θ2

=2A→sin∆θ2

For small ∆θ, sin∆θ2≃∆θ2

B→-A→=2A→∆θ2

=A→ ∆θ

subject: Physics
q: The density $\rho$ of a uniform cylinder is determined by measuring its mass $m$, length $l$ and diameter $d$. The measured values of $m$, $l$ and $d$ are $97.42 \pm 0.02$ g, $8.35 \pm 0.05$ mm and $20.20 \pm 0.02$ mm, respectively. Calculated percentage fractional error in $\rho$ is _______.
question: The density $\rho$ of a uniform cylinder is determined by measuring its mass $m$, length $l$ and diameter $d$. The measured values of $m$, $l$ and $d$ are $97.42 \pm 0.02$ g, $8.35 \pm 0.05$ mm and $20.20 \pm 0.02$ mm, respectively. Calculated percentage fractional error in $\rho$ is _______.
solution:
The density of a uniform cylinder is given by $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{4m}{\pi d^2 l}$

The percentage fractional error in density is given by:

$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100 = \left( \dfrac{\Delta m}{m} + 2\dfrac{\Delta d}{d} + \dfrac{\Delta l}{l} \right) \times 100$

Substituting the given values:

$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100 = \left( \dfrac{0.02}{97.42} + 2 \times \dfrac{0.02}{20.20} + \dfrac{0.05}{8.35} \right) \times 100$

$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100 = \left( 0.000205 + 0.00198 + 0.005988 \right) \times 100$

$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100 = 0.008173 \times 100 = 0.8173\%$

Rounding off to two decimal places, the percentage error is $0.82\%$.

Answer: $0.82\%$

question: For an experimental expression $y=\frac{32.3 \times 1125}{27.4}$, where all the digits are significant. Then to report the value of $y$ we should write
solution:
$y=\frac{32.3 \times 1125}{27.4}=1326.18$
So we need to report to three significant digit.
So, $y=1330$

q: In the density measurement of a cube, the mass and edge length are measured as <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>10.00</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0.10</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0.10</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0.01</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. The error in the measurement of density is:
question: In the density measurement of a cube, the mass and edge length are measured as <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>10.00</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0.10</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0.10</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0.01</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. The error in the measurement of density is:
solution:
Density ρ=massvolume
∆ρρ=∆mm+∆VV
=∆mm+3∆aa ∵V=a3
=0.110+30.010.1
=0.01+30.1
=0.31

question: The relative error in the determination of the surface area of a sphere is <math> <mi>α</mi> </math>. The relative error in the determination of its volume is
solution:
Δss=2×Δrr⇒ΔVV=3×Δrr

∴   ΔVV  =  32  ΔssΔVV  =  32 α

The diameter of the Sun will be
question: The distance of the Sun from earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and its angular diameter is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo>''</mo></math> when observed from the earth. The diameter of the Sun will be
solution:
Diameter of Sun is D=rθ

=1.5×1011×(2000)×13600×π180=1.45×109 m

subject: Physics
q: The current voltage relation of diode is given by I = (e<sup>1000</sup><sup> V/T</sup> - 1) mA, where the applied voltage V is in volts and the temperature T is in degree Kelvin. If a student makes an error measuring <math> <mrow> <mo>±</mo> <mtext>0.01 V</mtext> </mrow> </math> while measuring the current of 5 mA at 300 K, what will be the error in the value of current in mA ?
question: The current voltage relation of diode is given by I = (e<sup>1000</sup><sup> V/T</sup> - 1) mA, where the applied voltage V is in volts and the temperature T is in degree Kelvin. If a student makes an error measuring <math> <mrow> <mo>±</mo> <mtext>0.01 V</mtext> </mrow> </math> while measuring the current of 5 mA at 300 K, what will be the error in the value of current in mA ?
solution:
I = e 1000 V / T - 1 mA

When I = 5 mA , e 1000 V / T = 6 mA

Also, dI = e 1000 V / T × 1000 T · dV

                = 6 mA × 1000 300 × 0.01

                = 0.2 mA

subject: Physics
q: The length of a metallic wire is increased by 20% and its area of cross-section is reduced by 4%. The percentage change in resistance of the metallic wire is ________.
question: The length of a metallic wire is increased by 20% and its area of cross-section is reduced by 4%. The percentage change in resistance of the metallic wire is ________.
solution:
The given data is 

l'=1.2lA'=0.96A

Using the formula R=ρlA, the new resistance is 

R'=1.2ρl0.96A

⇒R'=1.25R

⇒ΔR=1.25R-R=0.25R

Hence, the percentage increase is 

=ΔRR×100=0.25RR×100=25%

subject: Physics
q: An experiment is performed to obtain the value of acceleration due to gravity g by using a simple pendulum of length L. In this experiment time for 100 oscillations is measured  by using a watch of 1 second least count and the value is 90.0 seconds. The length L is measured by using a meter scale of least count 1 mm and the value is 20.0 cm. The error in the determination of g would be :
question: An experiment is performed to obtain the value of acceleration due to gravity g by using a simple pendulum of length L. In this experiment time for 100 oscillations is measured  by using a watch of 1 second least count and the value is 90.0 seconds. The length L is measured by using a meter scale of least count 1 mm and the value is 20.0 cm. The error in the determination of g would be :
solution:
T = 2 π L g

∴    T 2 = 4 π 2 L g

∴     g = 4 π 2 L T 2

% Error in g = Δ L L 0 × 1 0 0 + 2 Δ T T 0 × 1 0 0

                   = 1 × 1 0 - 3 0.20 × 1 0 0 + 2 1 9 0 × 1 0 0

                   = 0.5 + 2 1 0 9

                   = 0.5 + 2 0 9

                   = 0.5 + 2.22

                  = 2.7%

subject: Physics
q: Vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> are perpendicular to each other when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>7</mn></math>, the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
question: Vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> are perpendicular to each other when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>7</mn></math>, the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
Dot product of two perpendicular vectors will be zero. Therefore,

ai^+bj^+k^·2i^-3j^+4k^=0

⇒2a-3b+4=0   ...Eq(1)

As given in the question,

3a+2b=7   ...Eq(2)

From both equations, we get a=1 & b=2

Then, 

ab=x2⇒x=2ab=2×12

⇒  x=1

q:
For the four sets of three measured physical quantities as given below. Which of the following options is correct?
i A1=24.36,B1=0.0724,C1=256.2
ii A2=24.44,B2=16.082,C2=240.2
iii A3=25.2,B3=19.2812,C3=236.183
iv A4=25,B4=236.191,C4=19.5
question:
For the four sets of three measured physical quantities as given below. Which of the following options is correct?
i A1=24.36,B1=0.0724,C1=256.2
ii A2=24.44,B2=16.082,C2=240.2
iii A3=25.2,B3=19.2812,C3=236.183
iv A4=25,B4=236.191,C4=19.5
solution:
In addition or subtractions, final answer must contain the equal numbers of digits after decimal as the adding or subtracting quantities among which is having the lowest numbers of digits after decimal.

A1+B1+C1=24.36+0.0724+256.2=280.6324=280.6

The Least number of  significant digit after decimal  in the above expression is 1 So, Sum should conatian 1 significant digit after decimal.
A2+B2+C2=24.44+16.082+240.2=280.722=280.7

The Least number of  significant digit after decimal  in the above expression is 1 So, Sum should conatian 1 significant digit after decimal
A3+B3+C3=25.2+19.2812+236.183=280.6642=280.7

The Least number of  significant digit after decimal  in the above expression is 1 So, Sum should conatian 1 significant digit after decimalA4+B4+C4=25+236.191+19.5=280.691=281

The Least number of  significant digit after decimal  in the above expression is 0 So, Sum should conatian 0 significant digit after decimal
Answer should be A1+B1+C1<A2+B2+C2=A3+B3+C3<A4+B4+C4

subject: Physics
q: If two vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> having equal magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are inclined at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math>, then
question: If two vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> having equal magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are inclined at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math>, then
solution:
The magnitude of resultant vector A→+B→ is given by, 

R'=A2+B2+2ABcosθ

Here A=B=R

Then R'=R2+R2+2R2cosθ

=R21+cosθ

=2R2cos2θ2

=2Rcosθ2

question: The angle between vector (A→) and (A→-B→) is :

subject: Physics
q: Two forces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>F</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>F</mi></math>, respectively, are at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> with each other. If the force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is doubled, then their resultant also gets doubled. Then, the angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is:
question: Two forces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>F</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>F</mi></math>, respectively, are at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> with each other. If the force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is doubled, then their resultant also gets doubled. Then, the angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is:
solution:
P→=2F,Q→=3F

R→=P2+Q2+2PQcosθ

=4F2+9F2+12F2cosθ=F13+12cosθ

If Q is doubled

2R→=4F2+36F2+24F2cosθ=F40+24cosθ

413+12cosθ=40+24cosθ

12=-24cosθ

cosθ=-12

θ=120°

subject: Physics
q: Two particles are located at equal distance from origin. The position vectors of those are represented by $\bar{A}=2 \hat{i}+3 n \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$ and $\bar{B}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 p \hat{k}$, respectively. If both the vectors are at right angle to each other, the value of $\mathrm{n}^{-1}$ is _____ .
question: Two particles are located at equal distance from origin. The position vectors of those are represented by $\bar{A}=2 \hat{i}+3 n \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$ and $\bar{B}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 p \hat{k}$, respectively. If both the vectors are at right angle to each other, the value of $\mathrm{n}^{-1}$ is _____ .
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=0 \\ & 4-6 \mathrm{n}+8 \mathrm{p}=0 \\ & |\overrightarrow{\mathrm{~A}}|=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}| \\ & 4+9 \mathrm{n}^2+4=4+4+16 \mathrm{p}^2 \\ & 9 \mathrm{n}^2=16 \mathrm{p}^2 \\ & \mathrm{P}=+\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{n} \\ & 4-6 \mathrm{n} \pm 6 \mathrm{n}=0 \\ & 12 \mathrm{n}=4 \\ & \mathrm{n}=\frac{1}{3}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: If $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ makes an angle $\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{9}\right)$ with each other, then $|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|=\sqrt{2}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|$ for $|\vec{a}|=n|\vec{b}|$ The integer value of $\mathrm{n}$ is ____
question: If $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ makes an angle $\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{9}\right)$ with each other, then $|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|=\sqrt{2}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|$ for $|\vec{a}|=n|\vec{b}|$ The integer value of $\mathrm{n}$ is ____
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \cos \theta=\frac{5}{9} \\ & \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{a b}=\frac{5}{9} \\ & a^2+b^2+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=2 a^2+2 b^2-4 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} \\ & 6 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=a^2+b^2\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & 6 \times \frac{5}{9} a b=a^2+b^2 \\ & \frac{10}{3} a b=a^2+b^2 \quad \& \quad a=n b \\ & \frac{10}{3} n b^2=n^2 b^2+b^2 \\ & 3 n^2-10 n+3=0 \\ & n=\frac{1}{3} \text { and } n=3\end{aligned}$
integer value $n=3$

subject: Physics
q: The area of a square is <math><mn>5.29</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The area of <math><mn>7</mn></math> such squares taking into account the significant figures is:
question: The area of a square is <math><mn>5.29</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The area of <math><mn>7</mn></math> such squares taking into account the significant figures is:
solution:
Given,
Area of square =5.29 cm2
Area of 7 square =5.29+5.29+5.29+5.29+5.29+5.29+5.29=37.03 cm2
{5.29 has 2 significant digits after decimal. Accordingly, the answer should also have two significant digits after decimal}.

subject: Physics
q: The acceleration due to gravity is found up to an accuracy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>%</mo></math> on a planet. The energy supplied to a simple pendulum of known mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> to undertake oscillations of time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is being estimated. If time period is measured to an accuracy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>%</mo></math>, the accuracy to which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is known as ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math>
question: The acceleration due to gravity is found up to an accuracy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>%</mo></math> on a planet. The energy supplied to a simple pendulum of known mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> to undertake oscillations of time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is being estimated. If time period is measured to an accuracy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>%</mo></math>, the accuracy to which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is known as ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math>
solution:
T=2πlg

Δll×100=Δgg×100+2ΔTT×100

⇒Δll×100=4+2×3=10%

E=12mω2A2=12mglA2Therefore, ΔEE×100=Δgg×100+Δll×100=4+10=14%

subject: Physics
q: A copper wire is stretched to make it <math><mn>0.5</mn><mi>%</mi></math> longer. The percentage change in its electrical resistance if its volume remains unchanged is:
question: A copper wire is stretched to make it <math><mn>0.5</mn><mi>%</mi></math> longer. The percentage change in its electrical resistance if its volume remains unchanged is:
solution:
Volume, V=A.l

In differential form,
dVV=dAA+dll0=dAA+dll

⇒dAA=-dll

Also resistance of wire is as follows R=ρlA
diffrentiation formula for resistance is as follows

 dRR=dll-dAAdRR=dll-(-dll)      
dRR=2·dll

dRR×100=2·dll×100dRR%=2×0.5%dRR%=1%

∴% change in R=2×0.5%=1%

subject: Physics
q: The maximum error in the measurement of resistance, current and time for which current flows in an electrical circuit are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>%</mo></math> respectively. The maximum percentage error in the detection of the dissipated heat will be:
question: The maximum error in the measurement of resistance, current and time for which current flows in an electrical circuit are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>%</mo></math> respectively. The maximum percentage error in the detection of the dissipated heat will be:
solution:
Heat generated through resistance is given by, H=i2Rt

Therefore, the percentage error in the calculation can be found by,

⇒ΔHH×100=2×Δii×100+ΔRR×100+Δtt×100

⇒ΔHH×100=4%+1%+3%

⇒ΔHH×100=8%

subject: Physics
q: In a simple pendulum experiment for determination of acceleration due to gravity <math><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, time taken for <math><mn>20</mn></math> oscillations is measures by using a watch of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> second least count. The mean value of time taken comes out to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The length of the pendulum is measured by using a meter scale of least count <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the value obtained is <math><mn>55.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The percentage error in the determination of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is close to
question: In a simple pendulum experiment for determination of acceleration due to gravity <math><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, time taken for <math><mn>20</mn></math> oscillations is measures by using a watch of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> second least count. The mean value of time taken comes out to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The length of the pendulum is measured by using a meter scale of least count <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the value obtained is <math><mn>55.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The percentage error in the determination of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is close to
solution:
g=4π2lT2
∆gg=∆ll+2∆TT
=0.155+2×130
=0.06848
Percentage error ≈6.8%

subject: Physics
q: A particle moves in $x-y$ plane under the influence of a force $\vec{F}$ such that its linear momentum is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}(\mathrm{t})=\hat{i} \cos (\mathrm{kt})-\hat{j} \sin (\mathrm{kt})$. If $\mathrm{k}$ is constant, the angle between $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}$ will be :
question: A particle moves in $x-y$ plane under the influence of a force $\vec{F}$ such that its linear momentum is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}(\mathrm{t})=\hat{i} \cos (\mathrm{kt})-\hat{j} \sin (\mathrm{kt})$. If $\mathrm{k}$ is constant, the angle between $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}$ will be :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}=\cos (\mathrm{kt}) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\sin (\mathrm{kt}) \hat{\mathrm{j}} ;|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}|=1 \\ & \because \overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{m} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \\ & \therefore \hat{\mathrm{P}}=\hat{\mathrm{v}} \\ & \Rightarrow \hat{\mathrm{v}}=\cos (\mathrm{kt}) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\sin (\mathrm{kt}) \hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ & \hat{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{-\mathrm{k} \sin (\mathrm{kt}) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{k} \cos (\mathrm{kt}) \hat{\mathrm{j}}}{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \Rightarrow \hat{\mathrm{a}}=-\sin k t \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\cos \mathrm{kt} \hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ & \because \hat{\mathrm{F}}=\hat{\mathrm{a}}=-\sin \mathrm{kt} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\cos \mathrm{kt} \hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ & \cos \theta=\frac{\hat{\mathrm{F}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{P}}}{|\hat{\mathrm{F}}||\hat{\mathrm{P}}|}=-\frac{\sin \mathrm{kt} \cos \mathrm{t}+\sin \mathrm{kt} \cos \mathrm{t}}{1 \times 1}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A torque meter is calibrated to reference standards of mass, length and time each with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo></math> accuracy. After calibration, the measured torque with this torque meter will have net accuracy of
question: A torque meter is calibrated to reference standards of mass, length and time each with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo></math> accuracy. After calibration, the measured torque with this torque meter will have net accuracy of
solution:
Dimensional formula for Torque

τ=ML2 T-2

Now the percentage error in torque can be calculated by

τ%=1×∆MM+2×∆LL+2×∆TT×100⇒τ%=25%

subject: Physics
q:
A particle moves from the point $(2.0 \hat{i}+4.0 \hat{j}) \mathrm{m},$ at $\mathrm{t}=0$,

with an initial velocity $(5.0 \hat{i}+4.0 \hat{j}) \mathrm{ms}^{-1} .$ It is acted upon by a constant force which produces a constant acceleration

$(4.0 \hat{i}+4.0 \hat{j}) \mathrm{ms}^{-2} .$ What is the distance of the particle

from the origin at time $2 \mathrm{~s} ?$
question:
A particle moves from the point $(2.0 \hat{i}+4.0 \hat{j}) \mathrm{m},$ at $\mathrm{t}=0$,

with an initial velocity $(5.0 \hat{i}+4.0 \hat{j}) \mathrm{ms}^{-1} .$ It is acted upon by a constant force which produces a constant acceleration

$(4.0 \hat{i}+4.0 \hat{j}) \mathrm{ms}^{-2} .$ What is the distance of the particle

from the origin at time $2 \mathrm{~s} ?$
solution:
As $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{ut}}+\frac{1}{2} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{t}^{2}$

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}=(5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}) 2+\frac{1}{2}(4 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}) 4$

$=10 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}+8 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}$

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{f}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{i}}=18 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+16 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$

$\left[\mathrm{as} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{s}}=\right.$ change in position $\left.=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{f}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{i}}\right]$

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{r}}=20 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+20 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$

$\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{r}}\right|=20 \sqrt{2}$
